chunk_summary: Baby-led weaning (BLW) supports self-feeding with safe textures, autonomy, and feeding skill development for children aged 6–24 months. It requires supervision, appropriate food choices, and excludes high-risk scenarios like dysphagia or aspiration risk.
learning_objectives: Understand the principles and safety guidelines of baby-led weaning (BLW).
Identify appropriate food textures and forms for BLW to prevent choking hazards.
Recognize contraindications for BLW, including dysphagia and neurodevelopmental impairments.
Learn supervision and seated feeding practices to ensure safe complementary feeding.
key_facts: BLW emphasizes self-feeding with soft, graspable finger foods alongside responsive spoon feeding.
Evidence shows no clear increase in choking risk compared to spoon feeding when safety guidelines are followed.
BLW is appropriate when the child can sit upright, bring food to their mouth, and is supervised within arm’s reach.
Unsafe foods include whole nuts, grapes, popcorn, and hard candies, which are choking hazards.
In India, safe BLW options include ripe banana strips, soft idli pieces, well-cooked vegetable batons, and softened roti in dal.
reference_quote: Evidence syntheses to date do not show a clear increase in choking compared with traditional spoon feeding when families receive proper safety counselling and avoid high-risk foods.
